sound_type: deep muffled booming -- extremely low-frequency ventriloquial sounds produced by inflating cervical air sacs with yellow skin, the most directionally ambiguous bird sound in the PNW; E=5
ecosystem: Mountain forest -- wet coastal conifer forest. Sitka spruce, western hemlock, western red cedar, Pacific silver fir. West of Cascade crest. Dense, wet, mossy. Reverse altitudinal migration: upslope in winter.
season: Year-round resident. Booming display April-June. Dawn and early morning. Reverse migration: upslope in autumn.
family: Phasianidae (4th and final entry. FOURTH AND FINAL PHASIANIDAE. Completes the quartet 153-156.)

coordinates.description: Sooty Grouse in wet coastal mountain forests of Washington -- Olympic Mountains, western Cascades. Sitka spruce, western hemlock, Pacific silver fir zones.

taxonomy_note.description: FOURTH and FINAL Phasianidae entry. Completes the quartet. Formerly Blue Grouse (coastal populations) -- split from Dusky Grouse in 2006. Yellow cervical air sacs (vs. Dusky's purplish-red). Coastal range west of Cascade crest. Most ventriloquial bird sound in PNW.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Phasianidae: fourth and final entry. Wild Turkey (153) + Dusky Grouse (154) + Ruffed Grouse (155) + Sooty Grouse (156). Quartet complete.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Dendragapus from Greek 'dendron' (tree) + 'agapos' (loving) -- tree-loving. fuliginosus from Latin 'fuliginosus' (sooty, dark) -- referring to the darker plumage compared to Dusky Grouse.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Dendragapus -- second entry. Dusky (D. obscurus, 154) and Sooty (D. fuliginosus, 156). Both formerly Blue Grouse.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Fourth and final Phasianidae. Coastal split of Blue Grouse. Ventriloquial booming display. Completes the loudest-to-most-mysterious progression.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Booming: approximately 50-100 Hz fundamental. Slightly lower and more muffled than Dusky Grouse.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 50-100 Hz. Extremely low. Less upper-harmonic content than Dusky = more muffled quality.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Booming: 50-100 Hz. Partially infrasonic. Wavelengths of 3-7 meters.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual note: ~0.3-0.5 seconds. Series: 5-6 notes over ~10-15 seconds. Longer pauses between notes than Dusky.
song_metrics.note_structure: MUFFLED BOOMING: Deep 'hoo' or 'boom'. Cervical air sac inflation/deflation with yellow skin. Helmholtz resonance. More damped than Dusky Grouse. Less upper harmonic content.
song_metrics.note_types: Muffled booming (primary, breeding display -- vocal/pneumatic), clucking (contact/alarm -- vocal), whining (female nesting -- vocal).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Series of 5-6 deep muffled notes with longer pauses between than Dusky Grouse. More deliberate pacing. Each note a presence rather than an event.
song_metrics.timbre: Deep, muffled, ventriloquial. More muffled than Dusky Grouse. Less defined edge to each note. The sound is ambient rather than directional. A presence that fills the forest.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=5. Moderate SPL. Effective 100-400 meters. The sound seems present throughout range rather than emanating from a point. Extreme ventriloquial quality.
song_metrics.tempo: 5-6 notes per series. Slower and more widely spaced than Dusky Grouse. More deliberate. Longer pauses enhance the mysterious quality.
song_metrics.context: Breeding season (April-June). Dawn and early morning. Male display from tree perch (often high in conifers).
song_metrics.regional_variation: Slightly lower-pitched and more muffled than Dusky Grouse -- a diagnostic vocal difference between the split species.
song_metrics.key_property: MUFFLED BOOMING -- 50-100 Hz, ventriloquial, most directionally ambiguous PNW bird sound, impossible to localize, yellow cervical air sacs, tree-perch display. E=5. FOURTH AND FINAL PHASIANIDAE. Blue Grouse split complete (Dusky 154, Sooty 156). Quartet complete.

plumage.overall: Males: blue-gray overall, slightly darker and browner than Dusky Grouse ('sooty'). Yellow cervical air sacs surrounded by white feather rosettes during display. Females: mottled brown.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: Males: yellow air sacs (diagnostic vs. Dusky's purplish-red). Slightly darker plumage. Coastal mountain habitat (west of Cascade crest).
plumage.seasonal_variation: Air sacs visible during breeding display. Otherwise concealed.
plumage.flight_pattern: Burst flight when flushed. Short distance. Strong but brief.

physical_description.overall: Length: 43-51 cm. Weight: 850-1300 g. Large grouse. Similar size to Dusky Grouse.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Slightly darker/browner than Dusky. Yellow air sacs. Coastal range. Wet forest habitat.
physical_description.field_id: Large dark grouse in wet coastal mountain forest. Listen for deep, muffled booming -- nearly impossible to localize. The sound seems to come from the forest itself.

nodes.title: Ventriloquial Booming -- From Everywhere
nodes.description: Most directionally ambiguous PNW bird sound. 50-100 Hz wavelengths (3-7m) diffract around trees. Minimal upper harmonics = no directional cues. Tree-perch display diffuses sound through canopy. Listeners cannot determine direction.

nodes.title: Blue Grouse Split Complete
nodes.description: Both halves documented: Dusky Grouse (154, interior, purplish-red air sacs) and Sooty Grouse (156, coastal, yellow air sacs). 2006 AOU split. Ruffed Grouse drumming (155) between them -- mechanical separates vocal pair.

nodes.title: Phasianidae Quartet Complete (153-156)
nodes.description: Wild Turkey gobble (153, E=6), Dusky Grouse booming (154, E=5), Ruffed Grouse drumming (155, E=5), Sooty Grouse booming (156, E=5). Loudest to most mysterious. Vocal-vocal-mechanical-vocal. Quartet complete.

nodes.title: Coastal Mountain Forest Ecology
nodes.description: Wet Sitka spruce / western hemlock / western red cedar forest. West of Cascade crest. Dense, wet, mossy. Reverse altitudinal migration upslope in winter. Conifer needle diet. Distinct from Dusky Grouse's drier interior habitat.

cross_references.relationship: SPLIT PAIR COMPLETE! Formerly one species (Blue Grouse). Dusky (154, interior, purplish-red) and Sooty (156, coastal, yellow). Both documented. Split complete.
GROUSE TRIO! Dusky booming (154) > Ruffed drumming (155) > Sooty booming (156). Mechanical separates the vocal split pair.
QUARTET BOOKENDS! Turkey gobble (153, loudest, 1+ mile) opens. Sooty Grouse (156, most mysterious, impossible to localize) closes. Volume to mystery.
FAMILY TRANSITION! Columbidae quartet (149-152) complete. Phasianidae quartet (153-156) complete. Two family quartets in eight degrees.
